caption: Figure 1: Examples of various segmentation tasks on heterogeneous document datasets.
llm_description: This image displays a grid of eight datasets used for document analysis tasks: Layout Analysis (PubLayNet), Historical Documents (MTHv2, SCUT-CAB-Logical), Scene Text Detection (CTW1500), Table Detection and Recognition (cTDaR-Modern, cTDaR-Archive), and Handwritten Documents (SCUT-HCCDoc). Each dataset is visually represented by its sample images with bounding boxes highlighting text regions or structural elements.

caption: Figure 2: Network structure of the proposed DocSAM. DocSAM unify various document image segmentation tasks into one single model through instance and semantic query decomposition and interaction. Skip connections and norm layers are omitted for simplicity.
llm_description: This diagram illustrates a vision-language model architecture for document understanding, featuring pre-processing of text and images, a vision encoder (Vision Backbone + Deformable Attention + FPN), and a multi-scale decoder with hybrid query mechanisms (Self-Attention, Cross-Attention, Feed-Forward) that jointly process visual and semantic queries. The model outputs predictions for mask regions, object classes, and bounding boxes from the input document image.

caption: Figure 3: Loss curves and on-the-fly validation during training.
llm_description: Training loss (left) and on-the-fly validation mAP (right) curves comparing DocSAM-base variants: baseline, curriculum learning, SimMIM pretraining, Sentence-BERT training, masked attention, query interaction, and query selection — showing varying convergence speeds and final performance levels.

caption: Figure 4: Qualitative results on public document layout analysis benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: A grid displaying ten distinct document layout datasets (BaDLAD, CDLA, D4LA, DocBank, DocLayNet, ICDAR2017-POD, IIIIT-AR-13K, M6Doc, PubLayNet, RanLayNet), each shown with its sample page featuring varied text blocks, images, tables, and color-coded regions to illustrate diverse document structures for layout analysis.

caption: Figure 5: Qualitative results on public ancient and handwritten document segmentation benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: A grid of ten annotated historical document images from diverse datasets (CASIA-AHCDB, CHDAC-2022, ICDAR2019-HDRC, MTHv2, SCUT-CAB, HJDataset, CASIA-HWDB, SCUT-HCCDoc), each showcasing dense, multi-column Chinese text with varied layouts and colored segmentation masks highlighting individual textual regions for OCR or layout analysis.

caption: across a wide range of document processing tasks, highlighting its potential for practical applications in various domains.
Figure 6: Qualitative results on public table detection and structure recognition benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: A grid of 16 labeled images showcasing diverse table recognition datasets and benchmarks, including FinTabNet, PubTabNet, ICDAR2013, cTDaR-modern, TableBank-latex, and WTW, each displaying unique table structures—from medical reports and scientific papers to handwritten forms and technical specifications—with varying layouts, languages, and visual complexities.

caption: Figure 7: Qualitative results on public scene text detection benchmarks produced by our DocSAM model.
llm_description: A grid of 16 diverse real-world text images showcasing various OCR datasets, including storefront signs, billboards, posters, and printed materials in multiple languages and fonts, each labeled with its corresponding dataset name.

caption: Figure 8: Failure cases produced by our DocSAM model. “GT” means ground-truth and “DT” means detection results.
llm_description: This image displays a comparative evaluation of six document layout analysis models (DocLayNet, M6Doc, CHDAC-2022, cTDaR-modern, cTDaR-archival) and four scene text recognition datasets (ICDAR2023-HierText, CTW-1500, ICDAR2019-ArT, CTW-Public, ICDAR2017-MLT), arranged in three rows. Each model is shown with its Ground Truth (GT) and Detected Text (DT) outputs. The top row features scanned documents with complex layouts; the middle row shows GT/DT for digital screens and signs; the bottom row presents GT/DT for real-world scenes like street signs, logos, and graffiti. The DT outputs demonstrate how each model segments and classifies text regions, revealing varying accuracy and precision across different visual complexities.
